biopsyNotes:
A. Biopsy
Clinical Diagnosis: Dermatofibroma
Location: Left Post Shoulder
Method: Incisional
Lesion Size: 1.3 - 0.6 cm
 The patient gave informed consent for this procedure. After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 1% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the area of interest. An elliptical incision was made using a Number 15 and part of the lesion was removed. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled A. #WC20-844. The wound was closed with Simple Closure. The site was closed with simple interrupted sutures.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Bacitracin was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.

 Informed consent was obtained from the patient. All risks, benefits, and alternatives regarding the survey have been reviewed in detail with the patient.

After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 1% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. An elliptical incision with the appropriate margins was made using a Number 15. Dissection of the surrounding/overlying tissue was performed to isolate, expose and remove the lesion. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled C. #WC20-846. 
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications and minimal bleeding.

After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol.  bacitracin was applied at the wound site and covered with a press dressing. Wound care instructions reviewed and given to the patient.

assesment:
Dysplastic Nevus -right cheek (MAD23-00197)
Management options discussed with patient: confocal, biopsy, excision, and monitoring. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed
Advised patient to come in at least annually for a full skin check. If anything changes then schedule appointment to be seen. OTC SPF 30 recommended daily.
Excision done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the excision. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever. 
SUMMARY:
1. Excision today.
2. -Sutures to right cheek removed in 7 days. 
3.  Do not allow the sutures to become dry. -Begin mupirocin 2% ointment to wound TID unit sutures are removed.
4. Post-operative wound care was reviewed with the patient and sent in patient packets.
5. Seek care immediately with any signs of infection including erythema, swelling, wound drainage or fever.
 
Lentigo maligna(Melanoma In Sutu) -left neck (MAD23-00198)
Excision done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the excision. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.  
SUMMARY:
1. Excision today.

3.  Do not allow the sutures to become dry. -Begin mupirocin 2% ointment to wound TID unit sutures are removed.

5. Seek care immediately with any signs of infection including erythema, swelling, wound drainage or fever.
6. FSE is recommended in 3 months due to patient's history of multiple Malignant Melanoma.

assesment:
Neoplasm Uncertain Behavior of the Skin
Management options were discussed, including: biopsy, confocal microscopy, watchful waiting. Risks and benefits of all options were reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
-Biopsy done today to left arm and left forearm 
-Patient verbalized understanding that the biopsy will leave a scar. Patient instructed to return to clinic with any signs of infection including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever. Wound care instructions reviewed with patient. 
Squamous Cell Carcinoma (MAD24-00677)
Considering the patients history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
 -Excision done today to right arm. 
-Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The site and any previous pathology associated with site (if applicable) was reviewed and confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection, bleeding, tenderness at site, and scarring. Other uncommon risks including nerve damage, temporary or permanent nubmness or weakenss of the the surgical area discussed. 
Patient opted to proceed with the excision. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.-Wound care instructions reviewed with patient. Patient will return to office in 14 days for suture removal.
Actinic Keratosis (MAD24-00678 and MAD24-00679)
 Discussed with patient that these are considered to be precancerous and are primarily caused by long-term exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun but can be caused from other sources. Management options were discussed, including: cryotherapy, topical therapies (5FU, imiquimod, etc.). Risks and benefits of each reviewed, with the primary goal of treatment to prevent the progression of the lesion. Patient educated on the importance of sun protection.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: 
 
- Cryotherapy done today to face, forearms, and arms. 
- Discussed cryotherapy procedure with patient prior to treatment. Potential risk factors associated with cryotherapy includes, but is not limited to: temporary pain or discomfort during the freezing process, skin discoloration/pigmentary changes, scarring, and recurrence of lesions. Patient is aware that more than one treatment may be needed. Patient agrees to treatment with liquid nitrogen today. 
- Advised patient that site will become tender, irritated and may come off. Patient is aware that additional treatments may be needed. If lesion does not resolve in 4-6 weeks, return to clinic.
 
Follow up: after 2 Weeks for Suture removal with BREE PARKER, PA-C

biopsyNotes:
A. Biopsy
Clinical Diagnosis: MM
Location: Left Neck
Method: Shave
 The patient gave informed consent for this procedure. After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 2% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the area of interest. The superficial layers of the site were removed by Shave method using a Blue Dermablade. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled A. #MAD26-159.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Bacitracin was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
B. BiopsyClinical Diagnosis: MM Location: Right Post Shoulder
Method: Shave
 The patient gave informed consent for this procedure. After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 2% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the area of interest. The superficial layers of the site were removed by Shave method using a Number 15. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled B. #MAD26-160.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Bacitracin was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
C. Excision Benign Lesion & MarginsClinical Diagnosis: L72.0 Cyst Epidermoid vs otherLocation: Left Mid Chest
Lesion Size: 1.5 x 1.5 cm
Excision Size, including margins: 2.0 x 2.0 cm
The patient was evaluated preoperative and noted to be in stable condition. 
 Informed consent was obtained from the patient. All risks, benefits, and alternatives regarding the survey have been reviewed in detail with the patient. 
 This includes risks of bleeding, infection, scarring recurrence of lesion and need for further procedures. 
After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 2% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. An elliptical incision with the appropriate margins was made using a Number 11 to obtain a full thickness specimen. Dissection of the surrounding/overlying tissue was performed to isolate, expose and remove the lesion. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled C. #MAD26-161. 
Repair: Complex Closure 
The wound was closed with a complex closure on 02-03-2026. The wound size with margins is 2.0 x 2.0 cm. 
Complex repair was performed because the defect extended into of the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial non-muscular fascia. Extensive wide undermining was performed to a distance of 2 cm (defect width perpendicular to closure is 2 cm) along the edge of the entire surgical defect to mobilize adjacent tissue, reduce wound tension and minimize the distortion of the adjacent functional structure.
Buried, absorbable retention sutures were placed to close the deep subcutaneous tissue and superficial non-muscular fascia. Retention suture placement was necessary: to avoid deforming, a depressed and contracted scar, anatomic distortion and functional deficit in adjacent fixed structures, injury to tendons and nerves in the area, to ensure proper range of motion, maintain function in the area, preserve the normal anatomy, reduce subcutaneous dead space to reduce the risk of hematoma, to reduce tension to skin to allow closure, and to reduce the risk of necrosis, infection and wound dehiscence.
The deep subcutaneous tissue, superficial non-muscular fascia and dermis was closed with 4-O Vicryl sutures followed by meticulous epidermal approximation.Epidermal closure was achieved with 4-O Nylon sutures.
Closure necessitated advanced layered techniques beyond a routine intermediate repair due to the defect size, depth, and location. The final closure size was 2.0 cm.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Bacitracin was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
